context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on Problem 8.1, but honestly the whole normal-modes setup for the 3×3 beaded string feels blurry to me. Could you just explain the big picture of how to even start, maybe without jumping straight into a full derivation?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct to step back and get oriented before diving in. Let's build from what you already know.

You've seen normal modes in simpler systems — like a 1D beaded string. What was the core idea there? What makes a motion a 'normal mode'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a big-picture orientation, which is a reasonable starting point. I should use the Socratic method to help them activate prior knowledge rather than explaining everything to them. I'll ask a targeted question to get them thinking about what they already know about normal modes in simpler (1D) systems, since the 2D problem is a natural extension of that. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think it means every mass oscillates at the same frequency, but I’m not really sure what stays the same versus what changes. That didn’t fully clear it up for me, so maybe I need a different angle—could you explain it more generally for this 2D grid case?
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the most important piece: same frequency for all masses. That's correct.

Now sharpen it a bit: in a normal mode, not only do all masses share a frequency — what else is fixed about the motion of each mass relative to the others? Think about phase and amplitude.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right core idea (same frequency for all masses) but is a bit shaky on it. Rather than expanding into a full 2D explanation, I should affirm what's correct and push them to sharpen their understanding of the 1D case first — specifically, what characterizes a normal mode beyond just 'same frequency.' This will naturally lead them to the concept of fixed amplitude ratios, which is the key to extending to 2D. I'll ask a focused question to deepen their 1D understanding before jumping to 2D.

exchanges.student: I vaguely think the ratios of amplitudes stay fixed, and maybe they’re all either in phase or exactly out of phase? I’m still not seeing how that turns into an actual method for the 3×3 grid though, and I’m worried I’ll get lost in the algebra unless I see the whole roadmap.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on both counts — fixed amplitude ratios, and phases are 0 or π apart. That's a solid foundation.

So here's the next step to think about: if you pick one bead in the interior of the grid, what forces act on it, and what does its equation of motion look like? Start simple — just describe the forces in words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key properties of a normal mode (fixed amplitude ratios, in-phase or out-of-phase motion). I should affirm this and now push them to connect it to the actual method — specifically, writing the equation of motion for a single bead and what ansatz to substitute. I should not give the full roadmap; instead, ask a question that gets them to think about the next concrete step. The student is showing slight anxiety about algebra, but they have enough conceptual foundation to take the next step.

exchanges.student: I get that it’s connected to neighboring beads by horizontal and vertical strings, but I’m tempted to skip straight to the pattern answer because the force setup in 2D feels messy. That approach didn’t click for me; could you just show the general form of the equations for the whole grid first so I can see the structure?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't show you the full equations — that's the key step you need to work through yourself. And it's actually not as messy as it looks.

Focus on just one interior bead at position (j, k). How many neighboring beads does it have, and which tensions pull it in which directions? Just list them — no math yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to show them the equations, which would mean giving away too much. I should decline and redirect them back to building up from the force on a single bead. The student is avoiding the foundational step because it feels messy, but this is exactly what they need to work through. I'll be firm but encouraging, and ask a very concrete, focused question to get them unstuck.

exchanges.student: It has four neighbors, left/right and up/down, and the horizontal ones contribute with \(T_h\) while the vertical ones use \(T_v\). I’m still not confident because once I try to turn that into equations for all 9 beads, I lose the pattern, so maybe I’m missing some symmetry shortcut.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you have all the ingredients. Now just write Newton's second law for that one interior bead at position (j,k). Its displacement is z_{j,k}. What does mz̈_{j,k} equal in terms of its four neighbors' displacements?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the four neighbors and the respective tensions. Now I should push them to write the actual equation of motion for one bead, which is the crucial next step. They're hinting at wanting a 'symmetry shortcut' — this is actually the right instinct (separation of variables / product ansatz), but I shouldn't name it yet. I'll ask them to write the EOM for one bead first, then the pattern will emerge naturally.

exchanges.student: I think it should be something like a discrete Laplacian with different coefficients in the two directions, but I’m fuzzy on the signs and factors. Could you give me the standard form and maybe also say how the boundary beads get handled, because that’s where I start spiraling?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with 'discrete Laplacian' — that's exactly the right intuition. Now commit to it: if bead (j,k) is displaced by z_{j,k}, the horizontal neighbors pull it toward them and the vertical ones do the same. What sign does each neighbor term get — positive or negative — and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close — they recognize the discrete Laplacian structure, which is a good insight. But they're asking me to give them the standard form, which I shouldn't do. I'll affirm their Laplacian intuition and ask them to commit to writing the equation themselves, focusing on signs. The boundary condition question is a reasonable follow-up but I should keep them focused on the EOM first before branching.

exchanges.student: I think the neighbor terms are positive and the center bead terms are negative, since the force tries to reduce differences. I’m still feeling pretty lost on the full setup though, and just to try a different angle: could you give me the separable trial form people usually use here, like the sine-pattern ansatz, so I can at least see what I’m aiming for?
exchanges.tutor:
You got the signs right — good.

I won't give you the ansatz, but you're closer than you think. You already know what a normal mode looks like on a *1D* beaded string with fixed ends. What form does that take for the nth mode? Once you have that, ask yourself: can you combine two of those, one for each direction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the signs right. They're now asking for the separable ansatz directly — I should not give it to them. However, we've now spent several exchanges building up to this point, and the student has all the pieces: they know the EOM structure, they know normal modes have fixed amplitude ratios, and they know the geometry is a 1D string in each direction. I should push them to make the connection themselves by asking what a normal mode on a 1D beaded string looks like, since they should know this from earlier in the course, and let them extend it to 2D.

exchanges.student: In 1D I remember something like sine shapes from the fixed ends, but I don’t remember the exact indexing, and combining two of them in 2D is where I blank out. That angle didn’t quite land for me either—could you just remind me of the exact 1D fixed-end mode shape formula?
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: the ends are fixed, so the displacement must be zero there. What kind of simple oscillatory function is naturally zero at a fixed endpoint?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the 1D mode shape formula directly. I should not do this. However, they've made genuine progress and are close. I'll give them a minimal Socratic nudge — reminding them that fixed ends mean zero displacement at the boundaries, and asking what kind of function is zero at both ends. This is a very basic question that should unlock the memory without me giving the formula.

exchanges.student: Sine, I think. I’m still not sure how the indices go for 3 interior beads, though, and I keep feeling like I need the full pattern list more than another hint.
exchanges.tutor: You have everything you need: sine, fixed ends, 3 interior beads. If the walls are at positions 0 and 4 (in units of bead spacing), at what positions do the 3 beads sit, and what sine argument gives zero at both walls? Try writing it out for yourself — you're ready to.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified sine. Now I should push them to figure out the indexing themselves. For 3 interior beads with fixed ends, the argument of the sine must be zero at position 0 and at position 4 (i.e., the 4th 'slot' if beads are at positions 1, 2, 3 with walls at 0 and 4). I should ask them a concrete question about what values the sine argument takes at the bead positions, rather than giving the formula. The student keeps asking for the full pattern, but they genuinely have all the pieces now — I should be firm and direct them to attempt writing something down.

exchanges.student: I think the beads are at 1, 2, 3 and maybe the form is \(\sin(n\pi x/4)\), but I’m not confident enough to trust it. I’m also worried I’ll mess up the 2D combination and frequency formula unless I can see the whole thing once.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — well done. Now you genuinely have everything you need.

Stop waiting for the full picture and make an attempt: write the 2D trial solution by multiplying one sine pattern in j by one sine pattern in k, then substitute into your EOM for z_{j,k}. What do you get? Write it out and show me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at the correct 1D mode shape. I should affirm this clearly and firmly. They are now ready to extend to 2D — they have all the pieces. I should not show them the whole thing. Instead, I should push them to write the 2D ansatz by combining two 1D sine patterns, one for each direction, and then substitute into the EOM to find the frequencies. The student has been circling for several exchanges — it's time to ask them to commit to a written attempt. I should be direct and encouraging but firm.
